nativeRange.habitat: The Dogfish Orientalis tends to inhabit deep inner to outer reef habitats and coral slopes of clear lagoons and seaward reefs, at depths of roughly 1 to 50 m. Juveniles are commonly observed singly on shallow protected reefs, while adults often gather in resting aggregations under ledges by day and disperse to forage over sand flats and seagrass beds at night.

diet.notes: In the wild, the Dogfish Orientalis has been documented foraging at night on benthic invertebrates including shrimp, crabs, polychaete worms, snails, and small fishes (trophic level approximately 3.9). Newly acquired specimens are widely reported to refuse prepared foods initially, so live saltwater feeder shrimp or freshwater ghost shrimp and blackworms are commonly used to trigger a feeding response. Once feeding, most individuals will transition to a varied carnivore diet of frozen mysis, krill, chopped marine shrimp and fish, and prepared carnivore formulas.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe, 4815x3611. Tack-sharp full-body adult in situ; vividly shows the diagnostic diagonal black stripes over silver-white body, yellow spotted fins, and yellow lips. Best single-specimen hero shot.
Bernard DUPONT, 4340x2604. Sharp, vibrant adult on the reef, clean side profile; whole fish in frame with all diagnostic markings crisp. Different individual/setting for variety.
Nick Hobgood, 1956x2200. Tight resting aggregation of several adults, sharp and colourful; illustrates the day-time group behaviour described in the care notes.

summary: The Dogfish Orientalis (Plectorhinchus lineatus) is a very large Indo-West Pacific reef fish known for a dramatic juvenile-to-adult color change, reaching close to 90 cm in the wild. It is generally considered an expert-only species due to its enormous adult size and well-documented initial-feeding difficulty.
careNotes:
The Dogfish Orientalis (Plectorhinchus lineatus), also widely known as the Yellowbanded Sweetlips or Lined Sweetlips, is one of the largest sweetlips offered in the aquarium trade, reaching around 70 to 86 cm in length. Adults tend to occupy deep inner to outer reef slopes and clear-lagoon coral habitat across the Western Pacific, from the Ryukyu Islands south to the Great Barrier Reef and east to Fiji and New Caledonia. The species is generally nocturnal, resting conspicuously in groups under ledges during the day and feeding on benthic invertebrates over open sand and seagrass at night.

Juveniles and adults differ so dramatically in coloration that they are sometimes mistaken for different fish. Juveniles tend to be solid dark brown to black on the dorsal half with three tan saddles and a tan ventral surface, transitioning as they mature to a silver-white body with a yellow mouth and fins overlaid with black dots and diagonal horizontal stripes from head to tail.

The most commonly reported husbandry challenge is initial feeding. Newly imported specimens are widely reported to refuse prepared foods on arrival, and live saltwater feeder shrimp, freshwater ghost shrimp, or blackworms are typically used to trigger a feeding response. Aquarists and specialists consistently warn that this species is prone to starvation if a live-prey acclimation period is not offered; most established specimens will eventually accept frozen meaty foods.

The species is not generally considered reef-safe: while it tends to ignore stony and soft corals, it has been documented preying on smaller crustaceans, fireworms, serpent stars, small snails, and polychaete worms.

A minimum aquarium volume of around 2,000 liters (approximately 500 US gallons) is widely recommended for adult specimens, with a tank at least 240 cm in length considered more critical than raw volume for a fish that can exceed 80 cm. The species holds an IUCN conservation status of Least Concern (assessed 2023). The trade name 'Dogfish Orientalis' is a marketing label used by some retailers; the same species is more widely known in aquarium and ichthyological literature as Yellowbanded Sweetlips or Lined Sweetlips.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Provided water parameters (72-78 °F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12), 500 US gal minimum, max size 34 in, peaceful temperament, not reef compatible, carnivore diet, expert-only care level, and live-feeder-shrimp acclimation guidance. Confirmed scientific name Plectorhinchus lineatus.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Haemulidae, Eupercaria), 72 cm TL max wild size, native range Western Pacific (Ryukyu Islands to GBR and New Caledonia), depth 1-50 m, wild temperature 24.4-29.3 °C, trophic level 3.9, nocturnal foraging behavior, and IUCN Least Concern (2023). Common name listed as Yellowbanded sweetlips.

additional.name: FreshMarine
additional.notes: Cross-checked aquarium care info: adult size ~86 cm, peaceful temperament, expert-only care, diet (live ghost/marine shrimp/blackworms to start, then varied frozen carnivore foods), and confirmed water-parameter ranges. Tank-size minimum listed lower (150 gal) than LiveAquaria's 500 gal; the larger figure was preferred per the playbook's tank-sizing bias.

additional.name: AquariumDomain.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank-size guidance (400-500 US gal with an 8x4 ft footprint preferable), shy/docile temperament, and feeding difficulty (live brine/glass shrimp commonly required to start newcomers feeding).
